edges.target: Absent or Minimally Ossified Vertebral Bodies

nodes.description: The metaphyses of tubular bones show characteristic abnormalities in SRPS Verma-Naumoff type, including round metaphyseal ends and lateral spikes.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000944 | Abnormal metaphysis morphology | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Vertebral body ossification is severely reduced or absent in SRPS Verma-Naumoff type, as part of the global skeletal mineralization defect.
nodes.id: Absent or Minimally Ossified Vertebral Bodies

evidence.snippet: HP:0004599 | Absent or minimally ossified vertebral bodies | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Congenital heart defects including transposition of great vessels, atrioventricular canal defects, and other structural anomalies.

Cleft lip and/or palate is a recognized craniofacial malformation in SRPS, particularly prominent in Type II (Majewski) where it is a characteristic feature, and also described in Type I (Saldino-Noonan). Oral clefts reflect ciliopathic disruption of palatal and midface development.

evidence.snippet: characterized by a narrow thorax due to short ribs, cleft lip, and/or cleft palate, cystic renal dysplasia, congenital hepatic fibrosis, pancreatic cysts, ocular and cerebral anomalies, as well as abnormal genitalia

nodes.description: Cleft lip, with or without palate, can occur in SRPS as part of the broad dysmorphic syndrome. Its presence helps distinguish SRPS from Jeune syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000204 | Cleft upper lip | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Hepatic fibrosis can occur as part of the visceral ciliopathy phenotype in SRPS, reflecting abnormal ductal plate remodeling.

evidence.snippet: characterized by a narrow thorax due to short ribs, cleft lip, and/or cleft palate, cystic renal dysplasia, congenital hepatic fibrosis, pancreatic cysts, ocular and cerebral anomalies, as well as abnormal genitalia

nodes.description: Severely shortened ribs leading to a markedly constricted thorax. More severe than in Jeune syndrome, causing perinatal lethality.

evidence.reference_title: WDR34 mutations that cause short-rib polydactyly syndrome type III/severe asphyxiating thoracic dysplasia reveal a role for the NF-κB pathway in cilia.
evidence.snippet: characterized by short ribs, a narrow thorax, short long bones, an abnormal acetabulum, and numerous extraskeletal malformations and is lethal in the perinatal period

evidence.reference_title: DYNC2H1 mutations cause asphyxiating thoracic dystrophy and short rib-polydactyly syndrome, type III.
evidence.snippet: Jeune asphyxiating thoracic dystrophy (ATD) is an autosomal-recessive chondrodysplasia characterized by short ribs and a narrow thorax, short long bones, inconstant polydactyly, and trident acetabular roof

nodes.description: Primary cilia are essential for transduction of Hedgehog signaling, which is critical for endochondral ossification and limb patterning. Ciliary dysfunction from IFT/dynein defects disrupts Hedgehog-dependent chondrocyte proliferation and differentiation, leading to severe skeletal shortening and polydactyly. The skeletal and organ phenotypes in SRPS are more severe than in Jeune syndrome, reflecting a greater degree of ciliary dysfunction.
nodes.id: Hedgehog Signaling Disruption in Skeletal Development

evidence.snippet: primary cilia play a vital role in transduction of signals in the hedgehog pathway that is especially important in skeletal development

nodes.description: SRPS are caused by mutations in genes encoding components of the intraflagellar transport (IFT) machinery, dynein motor complexes, and basal body proteins. These mutations disrupt primary cilium assembly and maintenance, producing structurally abnormal cilia (shortened, bulbous tips) or reduced cilia number.

evidence.snippet: 10 different genes have been identified as responsible for seven "skeletal" ciliopathies. Mutations have been identified in dynein motor (DYNC2H1), in intraflagellar transport (IFT) complexes (IFT80, IFT122, IFT43, WDR35, WDR19, and TTC21B) as well as in genes responsible for the basal body (NEK1, EVC, and EVC2)

evidence.reference_title: NEK1 mutations cause short-rib polydactyly syndrome type majewski.
evidence.snippet: absence of functional full-length NEK1 severely reduces cilia number and alters ciliar morphology in vivo

evidence.reference_title: WDR34 mutations that cause short-rib polydactyly syndrome type III/severe asphyxiating thoracic dysplasia reveal a role for the NF-κB pathway in cilia.
evidence.snippet: primary cilia in WDR34 mutant fibroblasts were significantly shorter than normal and had a bulbous tip

evidence.reference_title: DYNC2H1 mutations cause asphyxiating thoracic dystrophy and short rib-polydactyly syndrome, type III.
evidence.snippet: DYNC2H1 is a component of a cytoplasmic dynein complex and is directly involved in the generation and maintenance of cilia

nodes.description: Severe shortening of all long bones (micromelia) is a very frequent skeletal feature in the Verma-Naumoff type of short-rib polydactyly syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002983 | Micromelia | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Extremely narrow thoracic cage incompatible with postnatal respiratory function.

evidence.reference_title: WDR34 mutations that cause short-rib polydactyly syndrome type III/severe asphyxiating thoracic dysplasia reveal a role for the NF-κB pathway in cilia.
evidence.snippet: characterized by short ribs, a narrow thorax, short long bones

nodes.description: Polydactyly, typically postaxial, is a cardinal feature present in most SRPS subtypes. In Type II (Majewski), both preaxial and postaxial polydactyly occur. Polydactyly is typically absent in Type IV (Beemer-Langer).

evidence.reference_title: DYNC2H1 mutations cause asphyxiating thoracic dystrophy and short rib-polydactyly syndrome, type III.
evidence.snippet: short ribs and a narrow thorax, short long bones, inconstant polydactyly

nodes.description: Polyhydramnios is frequently observed prenatally due to restricted fetal swallowing and may be the first clinical clue prompting fetal imaging.
Postaxial (ulnar-side) polydactyly of the hands is the most common form of polydactyly in the SRPS Verma-Naumoff spectrum.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001162 | Postaxial hand polydactyly | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Renal cystic dysplasia as part of the multiorgan ciliopathy phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: WDR34 mutations that cause short-rib polydactyly syndrome type III/severe asphyxiating thoracic dysplasia reveal a role for the NF-κB pathway in cilia.
evidence.snippet: numerous extraskeletal malformations

nodes.description: Renal hypoplasia, distinct from renal cysts, is a frequent manifestation of the multisystem ciliopathy in SRPS Verma-Naumoff type.

evidence.snippet: HP:0000089 | Renal hypoplasia | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Lethal respiratory insufficiency results from the extremely narrow thorax and pulmonary hypoplasia, causing death in the perinatal period.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002093 | Respiratory insufficiency | Very frequent (99-80%)

evidence.reference_title: WDR34 mutations that cause short-rib polydactyly syndrome type III/severe asphyxiating thoracic dysplasia reveal a role for the NF-κB pathway in cilia.
evidence.snippet: numerous extraskeletal malformations and is lethal in the perinatal period

nodes.description: Pulmonary hypoplasia due to thoracic constriction leads to respiratory insufficiency and death in the perinatal period. This is the primary cause of death in SRPS across all subtypes. Unlike Jeune ATD, there is insufficient lung development for postnatal respiratory survival.

evidence.snippet: The affected child died in the neonatal period due to pulmonary hypoplasia leading to respiratory insufficiency

evidence.reference_title: WDR34 mutations that cause short-rib polydactyly syndrome type III/severe asphyxiating thoracic dysplasia reveal a role for the NF-κB pathway in cilia.
evidence.snippet: lethal in the perinatal period

nodes.description: Severe shortening of long bones (micromelia) more pronounced than in Jeune syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: WDR34 mutations that cause short-rib polydactyly syndrome type III/severe asphyxiating thoracic dysplasia reveal a role for the NF-κB pathway in cilia.
evidence.snippet: characterized by short ribs, a narrow thorax, short long bones

nodes.description: Short feet are a very frequent feature of SRPS, reflecting global shortening of tubular bones of the lower extremities.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001773 | Short foot | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: The thorax is severely shortened as well as narrowed in SRPS Verma-Naumoff type, contributing to the lethal pulmonary restriction.

evidence.snippet: HP:0010306 | Short thorax | Very frequent (99-80%)
